sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content:
This procedure establishes safe practices for the handling, use, and disposal of sharps; including hypodermic needles, syringes, lancets, scalpels, cannulae, and any other device capable of causing a puncture wound or laceration. Needlestick and sharps injuries are among the most common occupational exposures to blood-borne viruses in clinical practice.

This procedure applies to all clinical staff at FIFO mine site clinics. In remote settings, access to post-exposure prophylaxis and specialist assessment may be delayed; preventing sharps injuries is especially critical. Every clinician working alone or with limited support must be competent in safe sharps handling without reliance on a second person.

sections.title: Safe Handling
sections.content:
Apply the following principles during every sharps-related procedure:

- Never resheath a used needle using two hands. If resheathing is required (e.g., for intramuscular injection from a multi-use vial), use the one-handed scoop technique: place the cap on a flat surface, scoop it up using only the needle and syringe, then secure with one hand
- Use safety-engineered devices with integrated sharps protection mechanisms (retractable needles, auto-capping syringes) whenever they are available and appropriate
- Never pass sharps hand-to-hand. If a sharps item must be transferred, place it on a neutral zone (tray or sterile field) and allow the recipient to pick it up
- Keep sharps visible at all times during use; do not place them on a bed, chair, or under draping
- Announce verbally when handling or passing a sharp in a team environment
- Never bend, break, or manually disassemble used needles
- Dispose of sharps immediately at the point of use; do not carry used sharps across the clinic
- Never overfill a sharps container above the 3/4 full line (indicated by the fill line marking on the container)

sections.title: Disposal
sections.content:
All used sharps must be disposed of in an approved rigid, puncture-resistant sharps container that meets AS/NZS 4031 or AS 4031 (whichever applies) and is yellow with a biohazard label.

- Position the sharps container at the point of use before beginning any procedure requiring a sharp; never carry a used sharp to a distant container
- Seal the container when it reaches the 3/4 full line; do not attempt to compress or force further items in
- Label the sealed container with the site name and date of sealing
- Store sealed containers in the designated clinical waste area pending collection
- Arrange disposal through an approved clinical waste contractor; sharps cannot be placed in general waste or taken to a municipal tip
- Maintain a log of container sealing dates and disposal contractor collections
- Never crush, cut, or otherwise manipulate full sharps containers
- Never open a sealed sharps container

sections.title: Needlestick and Sharps Injury Response
sections.content:
Act immediately if a needlestick or sharps injury occurs:

1. Do not panic and do not suck the wound
2. Encourage bleeding by gentle pressure around the wound; do not squeeze forcefully
3. Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for at least 2 minutes
4. If the eye or mucous membrane is involved, irrigate immediately and copiously with water or normal saline for at least 5 minutes
5. Apply a waterproof dressing
6. Immediately notify your supervisor or site HSE manager; do not delay this step
7. Complete an incident report as soon as the immediate steps are complete
8. Initiate the blood-borne virus post-exposure protocol; contact the on-call medical director or nominated physician without delay
9. If the source patient is known and consenting, obtain consent for blood-borne virus testing (HIV, Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C). Do not compel testing; it is voluntary
10. Arrange urgent baseline blood-borne virus serology for the injured clinician (HIV, HBV, HCV): ideally within 2 hours of exposure
11. Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for HIV should ideally be commenced within 2–4 hours of a high-risk exposure and no later than 72 hours; contact the medical director to arrange
12. Arrange follow-up serology at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months post-exposure as directed by the medical director

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
Document all of the following:

- Every sharps injury in the site incident report system, including date, time, device involved, procedure being performed, source patient details (with consent), and immediate actions taken
- All post-exposure steps taken: wound care, notification times, medical director contact, PEP initiation if applicable, serology arrangements
- Source patient testing results (with consent) and injured clinician baseline and follow-up serology results; retain in the clinician's confidential occupational health record
- Container disposal records: date sealed, contractor collection date, manifest reference number
- Any near-misses involving sharps should also be documented via the incident reporting system to enable hazard identification and prevention
